title: Courting Disasters

jokes.text: The narrator's familiar 'Now, the story of a wealthy family who lost everything' opening monologue — a running gag repeated every episode
jokes.setup: Each episode of Arrested Development opens with the same narrator monologue summarizing the show's premise
jokes.punchline: The joke is that it never changes despite wildly different episode events — the repetition itself is the gag, and long-time viewers are in on it
jokes.explanation: The running-gag opening is a beloved institutional joke for fans. Its comedy comes entirely from familiarity and metatextual awareness — by Season 5 the audience knows the premise hasn't changed, which is itself the punchline. High economy and earned quality, moderate originality since it's definitionally repetitive.

jokes.text: Lucille to Lindsay: 'Oh! Look who thinks they can wear white.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay appears in a white outfit, apparently excited or proud of it
jokes.punchline: Lucille's catty remark implies Lindsay has no right to wear white (implying impurity/inappropriateness) — a sharp maternal insult disguised as observation
jokes.explanation: Perfectly in character for Lucille — her cruelty to Lindsay is one of the show's foundational dynamics. The line is economical and pointed, with the double meaning of 'wearing white' (bridal purity, fashion faux pas) landing as a layered insult. High character integration because only Lucille would open this way.

jokes.text: Lucille can't hear what anyone is saying 'over the sound' of Lindsay's singing, comparing it to a cat
jokes.setup: Lindsay reveals her 'talent' is singing and asks if the outfit looks good on her
jokes.punchline: Lucille says it's hard to think 'over the sound of... Was there a cat in here?' — implying Lindsay's singing sounds like a yowling cat
jokes.explanation: The cat comparison is a classic bad-singer insult, but the delivery via Lucille's feigned confusion ('Was there a cat in here?') elevates it. The structure of pretending not to know the source of the awful noise is a nice comedic choice. Not groundbreaking but well-executed for the character.

jokes.text: 'Most people close their eyes when they plug their ears.' [LUCILLE SIPS]
jokes.setup: Lindsay protests that it's 'not true' that her singing is bad enough to make people cover their ears
jokes.punchline: Lucille's follow-up implies people close their eyes to avoid seeing Lindsay too — adding insult to insult — then sips her drink with perfect disdain
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed double-insult: the first layer (ears) was established, but adding 'close their eyes' pivots the attack to Lindsay's appearance as well. The 'Lucille sips' stage direction is the perfect button — her physical indifference is the punchline to the punchline. Strong economy, exceptionally in-character.

jokes.text: Lucille reassures Lindsay: 'Maybe it'll be better if you don't win this one. And you wouldn't be losing much, just a cruise with your grandmother.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is ostensibly comforting Lindsay about having no talent for the competition
jokes.punchline: The 'comfort' is actually a dismissal — losing a cruise with her grandmother is framed as no big deal, which is backhanded in a Lucille-signature way
jokes.explanation: Competent Lucille-cruelty-disguised-as-comfort, but less sharp than her other lines in this scene. The joke is clear but somewhat predictable given the character.

jokes.text: Lucille describes Buster's disappearance: 'I took Buster off his leash, and I brought him down to the beach. Don't worry, I told him how to swim.'
jokes.setup: Buster has 'gone missing' and someone asks what happened
jokes.punchline: Lucille describes her adult son with a developmental situation as if he were a dog (leash) and casually mentions she 'told him how to swim' — implying she gave verbal instructions to someone who can barely function independently
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely excellent writing. 'Took Buster off his leash' treats her adult son as a pet, and 'told him how to swim' compounds it with absurd logic — as if verbal instruction is sufficient for someone who can't swim. It's dark (she nearly killed him), character-specific (only Lucille treats Buster this way), and quotable. Earns the 8.0 craft score through layered character comedy and dark subversion.

jokes.text: After Buster nearly drowns and is revived by Michael, Lucille says 'That was a freebie.'
jokes.setup: Michael saves Buster from drowning at the beach, apparently as an accidental display of life-saving skill
jokes.punchline: Lucille dismissively calls it a 'freebie' — implying it doesn't count as Michael's talent entry, coldly brushing off her son nearly dying
jokes.explanation: Economy at its peak — 'That was a freebie' is three words that reveal everything about Lucille's priorities (the competition over her son's near-death) and her personality. The contrast between the drama of drowning/resuscitation and her breezy dismissal is perfectly calibrated. One of the episode's strongest lines for sheer compression of character into a few words.

jokes.text: The talent show sequence with 'Balls in the Air' song playing while characters juggle, featuring the lyric 'When the foot's on the other shoe'
jokes.setup: An earnest talent show competition set to a custom song about juggling life's responsibilities
jokes.punchline: The lyrics deliberately mangle clichés ('foot's on the other shoe' instead of 'shoe's on the other foot') and the song's double entendre ('balls in the air') applied to an absurd family talent competition
jokes.explanation: The custom song is a layered joke — the scrambled idiom ('foot's on the other shoe') is a deliberate gag, and 'balls in the air' as a motivational anthem about juggling tasks has obvious double entendre. Classic AD dense joke construction.

jokes.text: Visual gag: Michael performs a magic/juggling act with confetti coming from his hat, and Lucille asks 'But where did the confetti come from?' — 'From Michael's hat. Honestly, these are so easy.'
jokes.setup: Michael is doing some kind of talent show act involving juggling/magic
jokes.punchline: The confetti reveal and Lucille/Gob's commentary treats the trick as trivially obvious, deflating any sense of achievement — Gob especially dismisses it as easy, which is rich given his own failed magic career
jokes.explanation: The rewatch bonus comes from Gob dismissing the magic as easy when he's spent the whole series as a struggling magician. Not a strong standalone joke but gains from accumulated character context.

jokes.text: Gob's magic trick punchword: 'Abra... cadaver.'
jokes.setup: Gob is performing a magic trick at what appears to be a competition near a coffin or dead bird
jokes.punchline: 'Abracadabra' twisted into 'cadaver' — a pun on the magic word that references death, fitting the morbid context of the scene
jokes.explanation: A tight, clever pun that works on multiple levels: it references magic (Gob's domain), death (the episode's thematic backdrop), and it's delivered with Gob's signature grandiose showmanship. High economy — the joke lands in one word. The portmanteau is genuinely inventive and quotable.

jokes.text: Lindsay returns for the talent competition and says 'And you never will. I want to save you the trouble of having to close your eyes while plugging your ears. So how's this for my talent?' — then tap-dances as a ghost
jokes.setup: Lucille earlier said 'most people close their eyes when they plug their ears' about Lindsay's singing
jokes.punchline: Lindsay has pivoted to tap-dancing (apparently in a ghost costume — 'Tap-Dancing Ghost'), callbacks Lucille's insult, and the new talent is described as potentially winning the whole competition
jokes.explanation: Strong callback structure — Lindsay directly references the insult to show she heard it and pivoted. The callback is clean and the 'Tap-Dancing Ghost' visual (inferred) is absurd enough to work. The fact that this apparently DOES impress people (vs. the singing) is a nice reversal.

jokes.text: 'Honestly, I don't know how Tap-Dancing Ghost doesn't take the whole thing.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has performed a tap-dancing routine in what appears to be a ghost costume
jokes.punchline: Gob sincerely argues the absurd act is the obvious winner — played completely straight, which makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: The deadpan sincerity of this line — from Gob, himself a performer who should know better — is charming and funny. 'Tap-Dancing Ghost' as a competition entry is inherently absurd and the line honors that absurdity without winking.

jokes.text: Michael accidentally saves Buster from drowning, and Lucille protests: 'This doesn't count! Who took off his gentleman collar?'
jokes.setup: Buster has nearly drowned and Michael saves him — the crowd cheers as if it's a talent act
jokes.punchline: Lucille objects to it counting as a talent (absurd in context of an emergency) and then immediately pivots to asking who removed Buster's 'gentleman collar' — treating him as a pet again
jokes.explanation: The 'gentleman collar' is a brilliant callback to the leash gag — it elevates 'leash' into something more absurd and period-specific, and Lucille's priority being the collar rather than Buster's near-drowning is peak Lucille. The line 'who took off his gentleman collar?' is genuinely quotable and character-perfect. Earns 8.0 through exceptional character integration and structural payoff.

jokes.text: Gob's incantation over the dead dove: 'Oh, spirit of this dead bird, fly into my dead brother!'
jokes.setup: Buster is unconscious/apparently drowned and Gob is trying to revive him using his magic/illusionist skills
jokes.punchline: Gob invokes a dead dove's spirit to revive Buster, treating this as a plausible resurrection technique — the dark absurdity of calling his brother 'dead' and genuinely believing in dove spirits is peak Gob
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy. Gob calling his unconscious brother 'my dead brother' with casual certainty, and summoning a dove spirit as a genuine revival technique, is perfectly in character. The dead dove callback (a series touchstone) adds a rewatch bonus. The absurdist logic is internally consistent with Gob's worldview, which is what separates great absurdism from cheap randomness.

jokes.text: After Buster is revived, Lucille says: 'Mommy's here. And as much juice as you want tonight.'
jokes.setup: Buster nearly drowned; Michael saved him; the family is relieved
jokes.punchline: Lucille's comfort is offering 'as much juice as you want' — treating her adult son like a toddler being rewarded with a treat, which also foreshadows the juice/Buster dependency themes
jokes.explanation: Juice as Buster's reward/currency is a long-running gag and this is a solid deployment of it. 'As much juice as you want tonight' from a mother to her adult son who nearly drowned captures the show's Oedipal dysfunction efficiently. Rewatch bonus for those who know the juice plotline's darker implications.

jokes.text: Lucille tells Dusty: 'Believe me, the guilt will fade. I mean, I've cheated on you a thousand times.'
jokes.setup: Dusty seems troubled by something (implied guilt about an unspecified act); Lucille is reassuring him
jokes.punchline: Her reassurance is that 'the guilt will fade' because she's 'cheated a thousand times' — casually normalizing infidelity as a life skill
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Lucille — giving terrible life advice with complete sincerity. 'I've cheated a thousand times' is hyperbolic in a way that feels entirely true for her character. Solid character integration.

jokes.text: Lucille to Dusty about cheating: '...it does almost make up for the permanent damage you did to my endocrine system.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is reassuring Dusty about infidelity being no big deal
jokes.punchline: She adds a non-sequitur about 'permanent damage to her endocrine system' caused by Dusty — implying their relationship has caused lasting physical harm, delivered as a casual throwaway
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'endocrine system' is what makes this funny — it's medical enough to sound real but vague enough to be absurd. Lucille delivering this as a throwaway while discussing the benefits of infidelity is sharp comic timing. The non-sequitur nature of the anatomical complaint is original.

jokes.text: The prosecutor cut her hair and the Bluths whisper: 'Does she look rattled to you?'
jokes.setup: Lottie (the DA) arrives in court; Lucille has been hoping to psych her out
jokes.punchline: The Bluths' entire psychological strategy apparently hinged on Lottie's hair — and now they're trying to read whether a haircut signals distress
jokes.explanation: A mild situational joke about the Bluths' delusional optimism about their legal prospects. Functional but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Prosecutor praises 'the most charitable, vibrant, balanced, beautiful woman named Lucille this town has ever known' — Lucille Bluth snorts 'Burn,' then clarifies: 'It's just that my mother's name is Lucille as well, so the burn was on her.'
jokes.setup: The DA gives a glowing tribute to Lucille Austero (the murder victim) in court
jokes.punchline: Lucille Bluth interprets the praise of another Lucille as a 'burn' on herself — then has to explain the burn was actually on the dead woman — the judge allows 'the burn'
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered joke with excellent structure: (1) Lucille mishears praise as an insult, (2) her explanation compounds the absurdity, (3) the judge solemnly 'allowing the burn' is a perfect button. The self-centeredness required to interpret a murder victim's eulogy as an insult aimed at yourself is exquisitely in-character for Lucille Bluth. The judge's procedural 'I'll allow the burn' is inspired absurdist writing. This is one of the episode's strongest moments.

jokes.text: The judge says 'I'll allow the burn.' — and proceeds with the trial normally
jokes.setup: Lucille explains her 'burn' comment to the court
jokes.punchline: The judge treats 'the burn' as a formal legal matter subject to judicial allowance — deadpan procedural absurdity
jokes.explanation: The judge's procedural gravity applied to slang is a great joke. 'I'll allow the burn' is highly quotable and works as a standalone. The institutional language applied to playground banter is sharp and well-executed.

jokes.text: Dusty's completely incompetent opening statement — stammering, voice cracking, laughing at his own predicament, 'I didn't even know you could use videotape'
jokes.setup: Dusty was supposed to be 'the only attorney who ever beat Lottie' — the defense's secret weapon
jokes.punchline: His actual performance is a cascading disaster: stammering, confused, laughing nervously, and then admitting he didn't know about video evidence — the gap between expectation and reality is the joke
jokes.explanation: Extended cringe comedy that escalates well. The buildup to Dusty as a brilliant attorney is efficiently done, and the collapse is thorough and earned. 'I didn't even know you could use videotape' is the comedic peak of the sequence — an admission so basic it's staggering.

jokes.text: Dusty: 'I feel like I got, like, totally the hard part of this case.'
jokes.setup: Dusty has just bombed his opening statement; the DA is strangely sympathetic
jokes.punchline: Dusty's self-assessment is that he got 'the hard part' — meaning defending a murderer — as if this is the unfair part rather than his incompetence
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the Dusty incompetence gag. His childlike complaint about getting the 'hard part' is well-observed character comedy — he's a man-child masquerading as a lawyer.

jokes.text: The DA (Lottie) whispers to Dusty across the courtroom: 'You can do it. You're ready.' — having slept with him the night before believing he was someone else
jokes.setup: Lottie and Dusty's professional opposition is undercut by the revelation they had a relationship; she inexplicably encourages him
jokes.punchline: The DA is coaching her opponent — a massive conflict of interest born of an accidental romantic encounter — played for maximum awkwardness
jokes.explanation: The irony of a prosecutor being so emotionally entangled with the defense attorney that she coaches him mid-trial is a solid structural joke. The setup is well-constructed even if the punchline is somewhat predictable once the relationship is established.

jokes.text: Lottie's pre-trial concern: 'Everything go okay last night?' Ron narrates that she had visited Dusty's dying father in the hospital, mistaking him for Dusty, and 'gotten a head start.'
jokes.setup: Lottie asks if everything went okay; flashback reveals she visited who she thought was Dusty in the dark
jokes.punchline: She had sex with (or foreplay with) Dusty's centenarian father on life support — the beeping, the tubes, the 'getting a head start' — and her actions flatlined him
jokes.explanation: This is a masterclass in dark misdirection. The layered setup: the dark room, the breathing, the 'getting a head start,' the beeping, the flatline — each element is perfectly ambiguous until the reveal. The narrator's clinical delivery of 'the tubes and other accoutrements weren't there for Dusty's erotic entertainment... but were instead the implements that kept Dusty's centenarian father alive' is excellent. Justifies 8+ for the structural sophistication and the sustained misdirection over multiple beats.

jokes.text: Lottie to Dusty in court: 'I've known you since you were 30! And you can call me Mom.'
jokes.setup: Lottie is revealed to be Dusty's stepmother, not his ex-wife; this is established mid-trial
jokes.punchline: She warmly invites him to call her 'Mom' — in open court, in the middle of a murder trial, in front of the gallery
jokes.explanation: The cringe comes from the inappropriate intimacy of a prosecutor-defense relationship going full maternal in court. The 'Mom' moment lands because it's so profoundly wrong for the setting.

jokes.text: Dusty asks: 'If the judge will allow it, permission to hug the defense counsel.' Judge: 'Hell yeah. Let's get the hug on.'
jokes.setup: Dusty asks judicial permission to hug his stepmother/opposing counsel in open court
jokes.punchline: The judge's response is 'Hell yeah. Let's get the hug on' — completely informal, enthusiastic, and wildly inappropriate judicial conduct
jokes.explanation: The judge's language ('Hell yeah. Let's get the hug on.') is the joke — it's the informal register of a bro at a party dropped into a murder trial. Following the procedural 'I'll allow the burn,' this establishes the judge as an absurdist comic character in his own right. Highly quotable and delivers genuine impact.

jokes.text: Ron narrates: 'It would've been a great ending for a feel-good movie, but it was a horrible beginning for a gruesome murder trial.'
jokes.setup: The prosecutor and defense attorney are hugging emotionally in open court while the gallery applauds
jokes.punchline: Ron contextualizes the heartwarming scene as catastrophically inappropriate for a murder trial — the gap between the emotional warmth and the legal horror is the joke
jokes.explanation: Ron Howard's narration is one of the show's greatest comedic instruments and this is a well-deployed example. The juxtaposition of feel-good movie framing against 'gruesome murder trial' is sharp and the delivery is built into the rhythm of the show. High economy in narration form.

jokes.text: Buster (quietly, about the hugging attorneys): 'Well, he is. Clearly is.' (about being called a murderer)
jokes.setup: Someone tells Buster to 'shut up, murderer' during the courthouse chaos
jokes.punchline: Buster quietly, matter-of-factly agrees that Dusty clearly is whatever he's being called — obliviously self-incriminating or missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Buster's oblivious self-incrimination is well-deployed here — quietly agreeing with the 'murderer' accusation with 'Clearly is' is darkly funny. The ambiguity of who he's referring to adds a layer.

jokes.text: Lucille to Dusty: 'That was humiliating.' Dusty: 'For my first murder trial, though, my first anything trial, not bad, right?'
jokes.setup: Dusty seems to think his disastrous opening went reasonably well given context
jokes.punchline: 'My first anything trial' — the reveal that he has never tried ANY case before, not just murder cases — the 'anything' is the escalating punchline
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'first murder trial' to 'first anything trial' is perfectly structured — the first part sets up the excuse, and 'anything' lands as the reveal that he has never practiced law at all. This recontextualizes everything that came before and is a genuine comedic escalation.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Oh, for God's sakes, stop talking like a child. I mean, how old are you?' Dusty: 'Fifty-six and a half.' Lucille: 'Jesus!'
jokes.setup: Lucille tells Dusty to stop acting like a child and asks his age
jokes.punchline: 'Fifty-six and a half' — he gives his age with the 'and a half' that only small children use when asserting their maturity — immediately undercutting Lucille's demand that he grow up
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The 'and a half' is the entire punchline — a single word that proves Lucille's point about him being childlike more effectively than any explanation could. The structure is airtight: she demands he stop talking like a child, he immediately proves he can't. 'Jesus!' is the perfect button. High originality for such a simple device, and it's earned rather than cheap. Lucille's exasperation is a bonus.

jokes.text: Dusty: 'Not gonna let that ruin my recess.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has told Dusty to 'pull yourself together' after his courtroom disaster
jokes.punchline: Dusty's response about not letting it ruin his 'recess' uses school playground language for a court break, completing the child characterization
jokes.explanation: A clean callback to the 'fifty-six and a half' joke — 'recess' as his word for the court break cements his childlike character. Very economical and well-placed. Not as strong as the '56 and a half' setup but a solid button.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Oh, my God, he's a motherboy.' — realizing Dusty is the son version of what Buster is
jokes.setup: The family discovers that Dusty is not the fierce trial lawyer Lucille described but rather her younger lover who turns out to be a dependent manchild
jokes.punchline: 'Motherboy' — a callback to the show's own running gag/episode title about mother-son codependency, applied now to Dusty
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes for long-term fans. 'Motherboy' is a loaded term in the AD universe — it has its own episode, its own cultural weight within the show. Applying it to Dusty in one word summarizes the entire dynamic and reveals that Lucille has a type. The economy is near-perfect (one word does all the work), the callback is richly earned, and the meta-referential dimension gives it maximum rewatch value. Lucille apparently serially attracts or creates motherboys.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'All right. I made an awful choice, but did I give you a hard time when you gave all our life rights to Imagine Films to make some TV show?' Michael: 'Yes, you did.' Lucille: '...That was somewhat amiss of you.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is trying to deflect blame by pointing to Michael's bad decision (selling family rights to Imagine/Ron Howard)
jokes.punchline: Michael confirms she absolutely did give him a hard time, and Lucille backpedals with 'that was somewhat amiss of you' — a massive understatement that is also the show commenting on its own existence
jokes.explanation: The show's metatextual relationship with its own production (Ron Howard as narrator/producer/character) makes this a rich joke. Lucille's 'somewhat amiss' is a perfect Lucille understatement, and the whole exchange comments on the show's own complicated existence. High character integration — Lucille's non-apology is textbook.

jokes.text: Michael's assessment of Lucille's bet on the DA calling a mistrial: 'She offered him gum.'
jokes.setup: Michael notes that betting on the DA calling a mistrial wasn't exactly good odds
jokes.punchline: The evidence of Lottie's compassion toward Dusty was apparently just that she offered him gum — the bathos of 'gum' as a sign of special treatment is the joke
jokes.explanation: The single word 'gum' as the deflating punchline is well-executed. Lucille apparently based her entire legal strategy on a piece of gum being offered — the absurdity of that reasoning is funny in a very AD way.

jokes.text: Lucille: '...I hired a completely incompetent lawyer and put my son's life in the hands of a moron, and I know what you want me to say, Michael. Okay, I'll say it... My bad!'
jokes.setup: Michael wants Lucille to apologize for getting them into this mess
jokes.punchline: 'My bad!' — Lucille Bluth's version of a formal apology for nearly condemning her son to life in prison is the same phrase a teenager uses after accidentally bumping into someone
jokes.explanation: The buildup to the 'apology' — complete with theatrical reluctance ('I know what you want me to say, Michael') — makes the deflation of 'My bad!' genuinely funny. It captures Lucille's complete inability to express genuine accountability while going through the motions. High character integration and economy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I don't think I have heard you apologize since... since people used to use that phrase to apologize.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says 'My bad' as her apology
jokes.punchline: Michael notes that 'My bad' is so outdated as an apology that the last time she apologized was when that phrase was in currency — implying she hasn't apologized in decades
jokes.explanation: A decent follow-up to 'My bad' — using the datedness of the phrase to imply Lucille literally hasn't apologized since the '90s is a good observation. Not as strong as the setup joke but a solid button.

jokes.text: Michael: 'It occurs to me that he may not come back from recess unless he hears a bell. I'll get him.'
jokes.setup: The family is worried Dusty may not return from court recess
jokes.punchline: Michael's realization that Dusty, like a child, might need a bell to know recess is over — callbacks the 'recess' joke while adding a Pavlovian/school bell layer
jokes.explanation: Strong callback that builds on the Dusty-as-child characterization. The Pavlovian bell implication extends the metaphor in a creative direction. Good economy.

jokes.text: Tobias offers to be a character witness and says: 'I know the Buster character better than anyone. I could play Buster with one arm tied behind my back. In fact, I've played Buster with two arms tied behind my back. I was handcuffed.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is being considered as a character witness for Buster
jokes.punchline: He escalates from 'one arm tied behind my back' to 'two arms — I was handcuffed' — with the handcuffed reveal suggesting his method acting has gotten him arrested while playing Buster
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. The escalation from idiom ('one arm tied behind my back') to literal ('two arms — I was handcuffed') is perfectly structured. The reveal that Tobias has been handcuffed while method-acting as Buster implies a crime or incident that is left gloriously unexplained. This is exactly how the show earns its reputation for dense, layered jokes — the punchline opens a door to further absurdity without closing it.

jokes.text: Dusty tells Tobias they don't need him to 'play Buster on the stand, more just talk about him as a person.' Tobias: 'Oh. Well, you don't think I'd be more appealing as the accused?'
jokes.setup: Dusty explains that a character witness talks about a person, not performs as them
jokes.punchline: Tobias's response is to offer to perform as the accused murderer instead — missing the point entirely while prioritizing his acting ambitions over Buster's legal defense
jokes.explanation: Tobias prioritizing his acting career over someone's murder defense is quintessential Tobias. The suggestion that playing the accused murderer is a better role than being a character witness shows his complete inability to separate acting from reality.

jokes.text: Dusty accidentally says 'the night of the murder' and Tobias corrects him: 'Alleged murder. Alleged.' Dusty: '...Yes, yes, alleged.'
jokes.setup: Dusty is prepping Tobias as a witness; Tobias is being coached on legal language
jokes.punchline: The defense attorney says 'the night of the murder' — a confession-level admission — and has to be corrected by the person he's supposed to be coaching on legal language
jokes.explanation: The reversal — Tobias having to teach the lawyer to say 'alleged' — efficiently shows how incompetent Dusty is while also being a good structural joke. The running 'allegedly' correction throughout the episode becomes its own minor gag.

jokes.text: Tobias insisting on performing in 'an English accent' for his witness stand appearance, then immediately correcting himself: 'Nay, nay, I shan't. I shall stick to the script. Not script. Vérité. I have taken the note... vérité.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has been asked to just tell the truth on the stand without acting
jokes.punchline: He spontaneously decides on an English accent for no reason, then catches himself, then uses 'vérité' (a filmmaking term) for 'just talking naturally' — every attempted course correction reveals deeper delusion
jokes.explanation: Tobias at his most Tobias — the English accent impulse, the theatrical 'nay, nay, I shan't,' then the pretentious 'vérité' as his word for 'being normal.' Each self-correction adds another layer of absurdity. High character integration.

jokes.text: Ron narrates: 'Well, "happily" might have been an overstatement.' — flashback to Buster complaining: 'I don't know the songs. You're a monster. You don't have to be any good.' / 'What?'
jokes.setup: Tobias testifies that Buster 'happily filled in' for his musical
jokes.punchline: The narrator undercuts with 'happily might have been an overstatement,' revealing Buster was miserable and Tobias's selling point was 'you don't have to be any good'
jokes.explanation: The narrator-as-fact-checker is a classic AD device, and 'happily might have been an overstatement' is a clean example. Tobias's reassurance 'you don't have to be any good' as a selling point for performance is excellent character comedy — he's simultaneously insulting Buster and being honest about his production standards.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Now, I do wish I could do this gratis, but I'm having a bit of a shelter crisis of late.' Then: 'Unrelated... What happens to this room when you leave? ... Yeah, I think they lock it up at night. Damn it. Damn it, damn it, damn it. Damn it!'
jokes.setup: Tobias has been claiming to need payment for his witness testimony
jokes.punchline: His 'unrelated' inquiry about whether the room locks reveals he was hoping to sleep in the courthouse — and his cascading 'Damn it's when told it locks is a private breakdown in front of strangers
jokes.explanation: One of Tobias's best sequences in the episode. The 'unrelated' pivot is a perfect structural device — everyone knows it's absolutely related. The reveal that he wanted to sleep in the courthouse is pathetically funny, and the 'Damn it' escalation into a private breakdown is a great comedic landing. 'Shelter crisis' as his term for homelessness is also characteristically Tobias — pretentious language for a dire situation.

jokes.text: Michael says he's 'free now so I'm gonna try to help out in court' and George Michael notes he found 'A Copy of A Few Good Men.' Michael: 'Look at that. How'd that get there?' Then reveals he's looking for his maritime law diploma as proof he passed 'what they call the wet bar.'
jokes.setup: Michael is looking for law books to help with Buster's case
jokes.punchline: 'What they call the wet bar' — the bar exam for maritime law reframed as the 'wet bar,' a pun on both the drinking establishment and the soaking-wet nature of maritime law — delivered with Michael's self-satisfied chuckle
jokes.explanation: A solid wordplay joke — 'wet bar' for maritime law bar exam is a pun that works on two levels. Michael's self-satisfied chuckle at his own joke is character-appropriate. The A Few Good Men gag is a nice visual throwaway but the 'wet bar' is the real punchline.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'The drop stairs were down, and you were yelling, "Where is it?"' Michael: 'Oh, that helps, too.'
jokes.setup: Michael claims 'father-son psychic connection' allowed George Michael to find him
jokes.punchline: George Michael reveals he just followed obvious physical evidence; Michael's 'that helps, too' concedes the point while saving face
jokes.explanation: A clean deflation of Michael's romanticism. 'That helps, too' is a good line that captures Michael's need to believe the best version of events even when confronted with mundane reality.

jokes.text: George Michael listing all the ways Michael has inserted himself: 'You dated my girlfriend. You moved into my dorm room. You bought my business.'
jokes.setup: Michael insists he doesn't 'get in the middle' of things
jokes.punchline: George Michael rattles off three specific examples with increasing absurdity — dating his girlfriend, moving into his dorm room, and buying his company — each one escalating the boundary violation
jokes.explanation: Excellent list joke with perfect escalation. Each item is worse than the last, and all three are callbacks to actual Season 4-5 storylines — the list works both as a standalone escalation joke and as a metatextual recap. 'You bought my business' as the final escalation is perfectly absurd. High structure and character integration.

jokes.text: George Michael and Michael discover that Fakeblock actually exists (sort of) — Michael tries the software and says: 'I cannot even get past the logo, so you're close.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is about to reveal that Fakeblock is entirely fake
jokes.punchline: Michael, in an attempt to be encouraging, says that not getting past the logo means George Michael is 'close' — which is both a terrible and sweet reading of the situation
jokes.explanation: A mild joke about Michael's relentless optimism — finding a silver lining in 'can't get past the logo.' Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Michael thinks George Michael is saying Rebel isn't over him; George Michael clarifies: 'No, Fakeblock. It's fake. There is no privacy software. I made the whole thing up.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks why George Michael won't sell Fakeblock to Rebel; George Michael says 'because she dumped me' and adds 'it's fake'
jokes.punchline: Michael interprets 'she's not over me' (about Rebel) when George Michael means 'Fakeblock is fake' — the miscommunication delays the devastating reveal and makes it land harder
jokes.explanation: Structurally excellent. The misdirection — Michael hearing 'she's not over me' when the real revelation is that Fakeblock doesn't exist — uses a classic misunderstanding structure to delay and amplify the actual bomb being dropped. The reveal 'There is no privacy software. I made the whole thing up.' is a major season-long payoff delivered with maximum efficiency. The structure is the strongest aspect here.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I do wish that I had not removed all of my privacy software and replaced it with Fakeblock, but on the other hand, it does explain why Pemender from Kuala Lumpur keeps signing into my LinkedIn.'
jokes.setup: Michael has just learned Fakeblock is entirely fake and he has no privacy protection
jokes.punchline: Rather than panicking, Michael finds a silver lining: it explains a LinkedIn mystery — the specificity of 'Pemender from Kuala Lumpur' is the comedic detail that makes this work
jokes.explanation: Michael's response to an existential security crisis being 'well, at least this explains Pemender from Kuala Lumpur' is perfectly in character — his need to find the positive in everything, combined with the hilariously specific detail of a LinkedIn stranger, makes this land well. 'Pemender from Kuala Lumpur' is a great comic name for a throwaway reference.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'The Chinese are gonna kill us.' Michael: 'I'll get us out of it.' George Michael: 'Please don't.'
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael have just had their heart-to-heart about Fakeblock's nonexistence and the global implications
jokes.punchline: Michael's reflexive 'I'll get us out of it' is immediately undercut by George Michael's 'Please don't' — summarizing the entire theme of the scene (Michael's help making things worse) in two words
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic economy — 'Please don't' is two words that encapsulate everything George Michael just said about Michael's tendency to make things worse. The callback is clean and the punchline lands hard precisely because it's so simple. The father-son dynamic is captured in six words total.

jokes.text: Gene Parmesan is disguised as the new bailiff; when called to the stand he's nowhere to be found; someone suggests 'Has anyone checked under the... new bailiff?' — Gene Parmesan is the bailiff.
jokes.setup: Gene Parmesan is called to the witness stand; the court notes he isn't there; someone suggests checking under the new bailiff
jokes.punchline: Gene IS the new bailiff — he was in disguise the whole time — Lucille shrieks in her traditional Gene-reveal reaction
jokes.explanation: One of the series' best-loved running gags perfectly executed. The buildup ('Doesn't seem like he's here. That's impossible. He's a private detective. He'd never be late.') followed by the under-the-bailiff suggestion is a beautifully structured reveal. Lucille's shriek is the traditional punctuation. The joke is elevated by the absurdity of a private detective taking a court bailiff job as a disguise while testifying in the same trial. High rewatch value as a series touchstone moment.

jokes.text: Gene Parmesan, after being sworn in: 'That never gets old!' Then: 'I'll tell you something else that never gets old. Whoops. I'm under oath.'
jokes.setup: Gene has just done his bailiff reveal, prompting Lucille's shriek
jokes.punchline: Gene comments that the reveal 'never gets old,' then starts to say something else that 'never gets old' — implying another lie or boast — then catches himself because he's under oath
jokes.explanation: Excellent follow-through on the Gene reveal. The meta-comment 'that never gets old' acknowledges the running gag while being in-character for Gene's self-satisfaction. The pivot to 'I'll tell you something else that never gets old — Whoops, I'm under oath' suggests Gene routinely lies or exaggerates and the oath is a genuine constraint. Well-structured and very in-character.

jokes.text: Lottie asks Gene about working at the donkey punch stand while 'undercover.' Gene: 'Definitely undercover. I don't need two jobs. [Chuckles] 'Cause everything's going really good.'
jokes.setup: Lottie implies Gene was at the donkey punch stand for legitimate undercover work
jokes.punchline: Gene's insistence that he 'doesn't need two jobs' because 'everything's going really good' is clearly false — his desperation to maintain dignity while obviously struggling is darkly funny
jokes.explanation: Gene's dignified poverty is a recurring characterization and this is a clean execution of it. 'Everything's going really good' delivered with a chuckle while clearly lying on the stand is very Gene. The self-delusion is charming rather than pathetic.

jokes.text: Lottie: 'What exactly is in a donkey punch?' Gene: 'You know, mostly Kool-Aid.' Lottie: 'So it's similar to juice, then?' Gene: 'It is.'
jokes.setup: The DA asks about the contents of a 'donkey punch' — which has an obscene slang meaning — as if it's just a cocktail
jokes.punchline: Gene's answer ('mostly Kool-Aid') and Lottie's follow-up connecting it to 'juice' (Buster's addiction) are played completely straight in court, while the audience registers the double entendre of the drink's name
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's most layered jokes. The 'donkey punch' name is played completely straight in court, the Kool-Aid answer makes it sound like a children's drink, and Lottie connecting it to 'juice' (Buster's weakness) — all without anyone acknowledging the obscene slang meaning — is perfect deadpan. The juice callback adds an additional layer for invested viewers. Excellent economy and originality in how the show manages to discuss this in court without winking.

jokes.text: Dusty asks Gene 'How many looks are you capable of creating?' — and then accidentally directs the question at the judge.
jokes.setup: Dusty is trying to undermine Gene's credibility as a master of disguise
jokes.punchline: Dusty asks the question to the wrong person (the judge) who has to clarify 'You asking me? I'm the judge' — then Gene reveals himself from another disguise as the actual answer
jokes.explanation: A double-beat joke: Dusty's incompetence (directing a witness question to the judge) followed immediately by Gene doing another reveal from yet another disguise. The judge's bewildered 'You asking me? I'm the judge' and Gene's reveal compound each other perfectly. Another strong deployment of the Gene gag with added Dusty-incompetence layering.

jokes.text: Dusty says 'as we've claimed' about Buster wearing the rock monster suit — Gene: 'Cause it was me.'
jokes.setup: Dusty is building an alibi argument that Buster was in the rock monster suit
jokes.punchline: Gene casually reveals he was the one in the rock suit — the alibi witness has just destroyed the alibi
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection — Dusty has been building an alibi and his own witness casually destroys it with two words ('Cause it was me'). The economy is perfect, and the dramatic irony (the audience may not have known Gene wore the suit) makes this a genuine plot-level surprise with comedic impact. The backstory (Gene took the costume and the job) is efficiently delivered through Ron's narration.

jokes.text: Flashback narration: Buster sees an 'old rival' at the donkey punch stand and flies into a rage: 'It's punch, like the juice! Oh, I need some juice.'
jokes.setup: Ron narrates that Buster ended up at the donkey punch stand and 'flew into a rage when he saw an old rival'
jokes.punchline: Buster's 'rage' is derailed by the word 'punch' reminding him of juice — he immediately abandons whatever rivalry he had to focus on getting juice — his juice addiction overrides his own emotional states
jokes.explanation: Excellent character callback — Buster's juice obsession is so overwhelming it interrupts his own rage. 'It's punch, like the juice!' is a good line because it shows his mental process. The callbacks to both the juice gag and the donkey punch/juice exchange from earlier in the episode make this richer for attentive viewers.

jokes.text: George Michael calls Tobias 'Uncle Tobias'; Tobias is tickling him: 'Tickle, tickle.' Narrator: '...back at the office, George Michael was worried about his dire situation... when an even more dire one came through the door.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is stressed about Fakeblock; Tobias is present being Tobias
jokes.punchline: The narrator's escalation — George Michael's situation is 'dire,' but what comes through the door is 'even more dire' — with Tobias mid-tickle being the comic baseline
jokes.explanation: The narrator's escalation joke ('dire... even more dire') is functional AD formula. Tobias doing 'tickle, tickle' at an adult George Michael is a good background visual gag but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Tobias explains that DeBrie blew their cover at the Spotted Palm 'by looking at her cellular telephone without holding it at arm's length and putting on her readers first.'
jokes.setup: Their undercover operation at the Spotted Palm ended abruptly
jokes.punchline: The cover was blown because DeBrie read her phone without her reading glasses — an extremely mundane, age-related reason for a spy-level failure
jokes.explanation: A good observational joke — the bathos of a covert operation failing because of age-related vision problems. The specific detail ('arm's length,' 'readers') makes it feel real and earned.

jokes.text: Murphybrown: 'Sorry, I keep forgetting that acting is my passion.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is explaining why Murphybrown/DeBrie blew their cover by not staying in character
jokes.punchline: Murphybrown's self-deprecating 'I keep forgetting that acting is my passion' is a funny inversion — normally people forget their troubles, not their passions
jokes.explanation: A quietly clever line — 'forgetting that acting is my passion' is an unexpected inversion. Most people forget their problems; Murphybrown forgets their identity. The self-awareness in the confusion is charming.

jokes.text: The troupe improvises an explanation for 'Annette's death' — she was 'gored' in the running of the bulls in a place where 'an animal took off with the body so fast you couldn't get it back'
jokes.setup: Maeby apparently 'killed Annette' and George Michael asks where they landed with that storyline
jokes.punchline: The group collectively improvises an increasingly elaborate explanation involving bulls in Pamplona — each person adds a detail to justify no body being available — the collaborative lie-building is the joke
jokes.explanation: The escalating collaborative fabrication is funny — each member of the troupe adds a detail that justifies the previous detail, ending with 'the animal took the body so fast you couldn't get it back.' The deadpan acceptance of increasingly absurd cover story elements is well-done.

jokes.text: Tobias offers to make the company a 'sexual harassment video' and adds: 'I'm no Harvey Weinstein, but I'm sure I could produce one.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to contribute to George Michael's tech company in exchange for lodging
jokes.punchline: 'I'm no Harvey Weinstein, but I'm sure I could produce one' — Tobias manages to reference Harvey Weinstein (a notorious sexual predator) while offering to make a sexual harassment video, without any apparent awareness of the irony
jokes.explanation: The Weinstein reference is dark and of its moment (Season 5 aired during the height of #MeToo discourse). Tobias's obliviousness is the joke — he thinks he's giving a reassuring reference point. The character integration is strong (Tobias as an actor in Hollywood is exactly the person who would know Weinstein and be oblivious to the irony). Slightly uncomfortable to score, given the real-world context, but the joke is clearly at Weinstein's expense via Tobias's naivety.

jokes.text: DeBrie: 'And I knew him. Did I tell you that story when Mr. Weinstein killed my ficus in the '80s? When he came over to audition me for...' George Michael: 'Yes. We all know DeBrie Bardeaux snagged a private audition with Harvey Weinstein.' DeBrie: 'Yeah, but I came on too strong, so I didn't get the part.'
jokes.setup: DeBrie begins telling a story about Weinstein; George Michael cuts her off implying everyone knows it
jokes.punchline: DeBrie's conclusion — 'I came on too strong, so I didn't get the part' — inverts the typical Weinstein narrative (where the predator 'comes on' to victims). DeBrie apparently was the aggressor and still didn't get the role.
jokes.explanation: Dark but cleverly structured. The inversion of the Weinstein narrative — DeBrie was the aggressor and still failed — is original and character-specific. The 'killed my ficus' detail is also a funny throwaway. George Michael cutting her off ('we all know') is a good structural beat. The 'some of us weren't lucky enough to even have an audition to blow' follow-up adds another layer.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Yes, well, some of us weren't lucky enough to even have an audition to blow.'
jokes.setup: DeBrie says she 'came on too strong' at her private Weinstein audition
jokes.punchline: Tobias laments not being lucky enough 'to have an audition to blow' — 'blow' doing double duty as 'ruin/fail' and its sexual meaning, in the exact context of a Weinstein audition conversation
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias double-entendre — 'an audition to blow' lands differently in the context of a conversation about a Hollywood predator's casting couch. The character's obliviousness to his own innuendo is a series-long running gag and this is a strong late-season example. The Weinstein context makes it particularly pointed. High economy — the double meaning does maximum work in a short phrase.

jokes.text: Murphybrown describing privacy software: 'It's like a dam, or like a stopper, or a barrier to stop people from...' George Michael: 'Like a block?' Murphybrown: '[stammers] Okay. [Laughs]'
jokes.setup: Murphybrown is describing his code as a barrier/block for data
jokes.punchline: George Michael completes his description with 'Like a block?' — which is 'Fakeblock' without the fake — and Murphybrown's stammering laugh confirms George Michael has unknowingly named the exact thing he's been lying about
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed irony — George Michael, who invented Fakeblock as a fake product, accidentally discovers that a real version of exactly that product exists when Murphybrown describes it. His instinctive naming of it as 'a block?' is the dramatic irony. Murphybrown's stammering laugh confirms it. The scene turns the entire Fakeblock plot on its head in two words. Justifies 8+ for the structural sophistication and plot-comedy integration.

jokes.text: Tobias explaining Murphybrown's privacy code: 'Nobody wants to be naked underneath their underwear. So, one adds an extra layer of protection. It doesn't have to be denim, per se, but everybody wants to hide their privates from something. Not everybody.'
jokes.setup: Murphybrown has explained a privacy software concept and George Michael asks for clarification
jokes.punchline: Tobias's 'explanation' is about being naked beneath underwear and hiding 'privates' — a metaphor so literal and so Tobias that it both sort of works AND reveals he has no idea what he's talking about. 'It doesn't have to be denim, per se' suggests he's thinking of actual underwear, not software.
jokes.explanation: Tobias taking a tech metaphor ('privacy layer') and translating it into literal clothing is vintage Tobias absurdism. 'It doesn't have to be denim, per se' is the comedic peak — he has gone fully literal. 'Not everybody' as a self-correction is also funny. High character integration.

jokes.text: The warden says he got information 'straight from the horse's mouth.' Lottie asks: 'And who is the horse?' The warden pauses and says: 'Lucille Bluth, of course, of course.'
jokes.setup: The warden uses the idiom 'straight from the horse's mouth'; Lottie asks who the horse is
jokes.punchline: 'Lucille Bluth, of course, of course' — the 'of course, of course' echoes the Mr. Ed theme song ('A horse is a horse, of course, of course'), calling Lucille a horse while using a rhyme scheme from a classic TV show
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best jokes in the episode and possibly a top-tier AD wordplay moment. The setup ('straight from the horse's mouth') is so familiar it's invisible — then 'who is the horse?' reactivates it — and 'Lucille Bluth, of course, of course' lands the Mr. Ed reference while insulting Lucille in the most Arrested Development way possible. The triple 'of course' rhyme scheme, the Mr. Ed callback, the Lucille insult, and the warden's dramatic pause all combine into something genuinely impressive. Highly quotable, rewatch-essential.

jokes.text: 'All seals can come loose with a little bit of... Gentles' pressuring.'
jokes.setup: Lottie notes the case information had been sealed; the warden explains how he got it
jokes.punchline: The warden's surname 'Gentles' is turned into a verb — 'Gentles' pressuring' — a self-aggrandizing euphemism for whatever intimidation he used to get the information
jokes.explanation: Using 'Gentles' as a verb for pressure/interrogation is a clever name-based wordplay. The irony of a man named 'Gentles' who apparently intimidates people into revealing sealed documents is a nice structural layer.

jokes.text: Ron narrates: 'The only thing guarded that evening was Warden Gentles' private trailer.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has gotten Buster released through a dinner with Warden Gentles that turned intimate, during which she revealed damaging information
jokes.punchline: Ron's summary — that Gentles' trailer was the only 'guarded' thing — implies Lucille was completely unguarded with her information while Gentles protected his own interests
jokes.explanation: A solid Ron narration joke — 'private trailer' doing double duty as his literal residence and his privacy. The irony of a warden being the one who maneuvers Lucille is well-observed.

jokes.text: Ron Howard breaking the fourth wall mid-trial to apologize for the footage quality: 'Now, remember, this is really rough. And our location guy was only able to rent out the cottage for shooting for, like, two days. And it's all temp sound. We haven't licensed most of this music.'
jokes.setup: The prosecutor plays a clip from the Imagine Bluth streaming project (Ron Howard's documentary) as evidence
jokes.punchline: Ron Howard/the narrator immediately breaks into apologetic producer mode, addressing the audience/court about production quality — he's simultaneously the narrator, a character, and a Hollywood producer concerned about his rough cut being shown publicly
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most AD-specific jokes — Ron Howard switching from omniscient narrator to flustered producer apologizing for temp sound and unlicensed music. The layers are precise: he's apologizing to the courtroom audience which is also the show's audience. 'We haven't licensed most of this music' is a genuine real-world production detail applied to a fictional context. High originality and character integration — only AD does this specific kind of meta humor.

jokes.text: Ron Howard, after the flashback: 'You know, it's funny, I was actually worried about them showing this in court, but it turned out better than I'd remembered. And I actually think it's a good advertisement for the show. Of course, we still haven't gotten Brian's notes. And obviously, we're gonna put Jean Smart's face on the stuntman.'
jokes.setup: The court has just watched damning footage from Ron's project
jokes.punchline: Ron is concerned only with the quality of his film — not that it may send Buster to prison — and notes they still need Brian Grazer's notes and will need to CGI Jean Smart's face onto a stuntman
jokes.explanation: Continuation of the Ron-as-Hollywood-producer joke — his priority is his rough cut's quality and Brian Grazer's notes, not the legal proceedings destroying the family. 'Jean Smart's face on the stuntman' is a very specific real production detail that grounds the absurdity. The self-centeredness mirrors the Bluths' own priorities.

jokes.text: Dusty, shocked by the video evidence: 'My dad made us give up Netflix when they went up by two dollars.'
jokes.setup: Dusty says he didn't know about the Imagine project video
jokes.punchline: The reason he didn't know about a Netflix streaming series is that his family cancelled Netflix to save $2/month — both explaining his ignorance and reinforcing his childlike dependence on his father's decisions
jokes.explanation: A great Dusty characterization joke — a 56-year-old man still subject to his father's Netflix decisions. The $2 price increase detail is extremely specific and relatable, making the absurdity land harder. This both explains the plot and reinforces his man-child status.

jokes.text: Warden Gentles: 'I'm not just saying this to suck up to Ron Howard, but I think we nailed it.' (about the footage quality of the Imagine project)
jokes.setup: The warden is testifying about the accuracy of the Imagine project's depiction
jokes.punchline: He spontaneously adds 'I'm not just saying this to suck up to Ron Howard' — acknowledging that Ron Howard (the narrator) is watching and might be flattered — collapsing the fiction/reality boundary
jokes.explanation: The warden acknowledging Ron Howard's presence as a real person he might want to impress is a sharp fourth-wall moment. The 'I'm not just saying this to suck up' qualifier implies everyone on the show is aware of Ron Howard watching them — and might be performing for him. Good meta comedy with real wit behind it.

jokes.text: After the devastating video evidence, Dusty shouts: 'Mistrial! Mistrial!' — then Buster responds: 'Yes, yes! I missed your trial!'
jokes.setup: Dusty is desperately calling for a mistrial after the evidence
jokes.punchline: Buster mishears 'mistrial' as 'missed your trial' and confirms affectionately — his hearing/comprehension is so impaired (or his understanding of his own situation so minimal) that this wordplay happens naturally
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best wordplay jokes. The homophone 'mistrial/missed your trial' is perfectly set up by the actual dramatic context — Dusty screaming for a mistrial — and Buster's benign misunderstanding is completely in character. Only Buster would hear this and respond warmly. The joke works on multiple levels: it's funny as wordplay, it reveals Buster's disconnect from his own legal jeopardy, and 'I missed your trial' is a sweet/oblivious sentiment. Very quotable and high rewatch value.

jokes.text: Lucille confronts Dusty: 'So you sell me out to Warden Gentles?' Dusty: 'And we both know you said I wouldn't get in trouble. You made a promise!' Lucille: 'No, Buster, it was a mistake!'
jokes.setup: In the chaos after devastating testimony, Lucille confronts Dusty then turns to Buster
jokes.punchline: Lucille's 'No, Buster, it was a mistake!' in response to the accusation — she's simultaneously addressing Buster and admitting her whole approach to the trial was a mistake, with 'it' doing double duty
jokes.explanation: The ambiguity of Lucille's 'it was a mistake' — referring both to her hiring of Dusty and to being Buster's mother — is a nice dark character beat, though the chaos of the scene makes it somewhat hard to parse on first watch.

jokes.text: Lucille to Dusty: 'So you sell me out to Warden Gentles? And we both know you said I wouldn't get in trouble.' Dusty: 'No, Buster, it was a mistake!' Lucille: 'You can live with her, Dusty. She can be your mother.'
jokes.setup: Everyone is furious after the devastating court session; Lucille is attacking Dusty
jokes.punchline: Lucille's ultimate punishment for Dusty is telling him to 'live with' Lottie — framing the stepmom/mother figure as a curse — while also acknowledging out loud that she knows Dusty needs a mother figure
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the motherboy dynamic — Lucille weaponizes the mother relationship by offering Dusty a different mother. The irony that she's essentially confirming the motherboy accusation while using it as a punishment is sharp. Lucille's cruelty is finely calibrated.

jokes.text: Lottie to Dusty after the trial disaster: 'Now you made it almost impossible for me to win this case. I mean, was that an option? Because you did almost kill my dad.'
jokes.setup: Lottie is furious that Dusty's incompetence may have hurt the prosecution; she starts to scold him
jokes.punchline: She pivots from professional outrage to 'was that an option? Because you did almost kill my dad' — referencing (obliquely) the flatline incident with Dusty's centenarian father, suggesting it's relevant leverage
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the centenarian father flatline — 'you did almost kill my dad' references Lucille's inadvertent involvement in a way that recontextualizes Lottie's sympathy toward Dusty throughout the trial. The dark pivot from professional to personal is well-structured.

jokes.text: On the Next AD: Murphybrown demonstrates real privacy software ('they'll see dummy info instead of your real data') and asks 'So, like, do you want to watch Gone Girl?' Then reveals they can watch it for free because the studios will just see dummy data.
jokes.setup: Murphybrown has real privacy/spoofing software and is demonstrating it to George Michael (who needs Fakeblock to exist)
jokes.punchline: The demonstration of serious privacy software is immediately applied to pirating Gone Girl — from corporate espionage protection to streaming piracy in one pivot
jokes.explanation: The bathos of serious corporate privacy software being immediately used to pirate Gone Girl is well-executed. It's also a plot-relevant joke — the accidental Fakeblock solution arriving through piracy is perfectly absurd AD storytelling.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Well, this is Fakeblock.' — realizing the piracy software IS Fakeblock
jokes.setup: George Michael has been building Fakeblock (a fictional privacy software) for years, only to just confess it doesn't exist
jokes.punchline: Murphybrown's piracy/privacy software turns out to be functionally identical to what Fakeblock was supposed to be — George Michael's years-long fraud accidentally becomes real via a completely unrelated person
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest structural jokes and a major season-long payoff. The irony that Fakeblock accidentally becomes real — not through George Michael's effort, but through a homeless teenager's piracy tool — is perfect AD plotting. The whole joke lands in four words: 'Well, this is Fakeblock.' High originality for the meta-fictional-becomes-real twist, impeccable structure as a season-long callback, and deeply earned. This is the kind of storytelling that makes AD rewatchable.

jokes.text: Murphybrown: 'Hey, do you want to see Ben Affleck's peen?' George Michael (stammering): 'Yeah. Would love to.' Then both look at the laptop with increasing 'Huh. Yep, yep' reactions.
jokes.setup: They've just discovered Fakeblock works; the celebration takes an unexpected turn
jokes.punchline: Murphybrown pivots to offering to show Ben Affleck's 'peen' — apparently accessible through the privacy-breaching software — and George Michael's stammered 'Yeah. Would love to' followed by 'Huh. Yep, yep' reactions suggest they are genuinely looking at it
jokes.explanation: A good button to the Fakeblock reveal — the software's first application is accessing a celebrity's private photos, which is both absurd and plausible. George Michael's reluctant 'Would love to' and the mutual 'Huh. Yep, yep' reactions are a funny visual joke inferred from dialogue. The escalating 'Yep, yep, yep' suggests they're scrolling with increasing appreciation.

tmdb_overview: George and Lucille overestimate their new lawyer's expertise in the courtroom. Two familiar faces testify, and a video sheds more light on the case.
insight: Cringe-driven finale lands 61 jokes in tight legal chaos—Season 5's densest hour.
